text: The laparoscopic view shows the sigmoid colon protruding through the indirect inguinal hernia. The surgeon applies external manual pressure to reduce the hernia, partially reducing the sigmoid colon, but a tennis ball-sized portion remains stuck in the groin.

The robotic system is docked, with a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and scissors in the right hand. The surgeon demonstrates the absence of a left inguinal hernia. A small red dot is visible in the preperitoneal space near the ASIS due to the injection of ten cc's of a quarter percent marking solution. The surgeon begins creating the peritoneal flap without attempting to reduce the bowel robotically.

The surgeon identifies the inferior epigastric vessels in a very thin male patient, avoiding entry into the retro-rectal space due to the clear visibility of these vessels and minimal surrounding fat.

The surgeon begins creating a peritoneal flap, carefully staying close to the peritoneum in the Bagros space to facilitate dissection of the testicular vessels. The visual field includes bowel and sigmoid retroperitoneal fat, requiring cautious manipulation due to the absence of lateral peritoneum.

The surgeon achieves hemostasis, revealing the differentiation between the tissue associated with the testicular vessels and the colon. The transverse salus sling is visible as a robust structure anchoring the mesh in place. The surgeon attempts to mobilize the sigmoid colon by removing attachments from the sling, but the tissue remains firmly adhered.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the tissue, avoiding excessive force to prevent tearing. Once the transvascular sling is freed, a small wad of tissue is retracted by the bedside assistant. A portion of the colon remains adhered, requiring detachment of retroperitoneal attachments to the testicular vessels for complete reduction.
The surgeon dissects the space of Retzius, aiming to visualize the superior pubic rami. The area contains some adipose tissue.

The surgeon remains anterior to the retro-rectus fat, encountering a significant amount of adipose tissue despite the patient's thin physique. The anterior tissue, including attachments from the transverse salus sling and transverse abdominus muscle, visibly holds the sac in place, requiring careful dissection to release these attachments.

The surgeon carefully dissects the anterior medial attachments of the hernia sac using cautery, while avoiding thermal injury to the nearby colon. Thick bands of scar tissue are visible, resulting from chronic sliding of the hernia over decades. The reflection of the transverse salus fascia can be seen during the dissection.

The surgeon separates the testicular vessels from surrounding tissue, carefully detaching them from the hernia sac and colon. This maneuver increases the mobility of the sac and colon, facilitating the reduction of the incarcerated hernia. The use of Firefly technology aids in identifying the testicular vessels before potential bleeding occurs.

The surgeon creates a large pocket for mesh placement, ensuring good posterior lateral mesh overlap. The testicular vessels are visible, separated from the sigmoid colon to prevent entanglement during hernia reduction. Additional attachments are divided, allowing structures to be released without external pressure.

The surgeon identifies the testicular vessels and dissects along the plane between the hernia sac and these vessels. Scar tissue is visible from the hernia sac pivoting around the edge. The surgeon carefully dissects the sac off the testicular vessels and vas deferens, ensuring no injury to large or small vessels to maintain a bloodless field.

The surgeon uses cautery to achieve hemostasis, carefully avoiding the vas deferens, which is close to the surgical field. The pocket is developed by dissecting two centimeters deep to achieve the critical view of the myopectomy orifice, extending the dissection across the midline by at least two centimeters, while ensuring the cord is paralyzed.

The surgeon meticulously dissects peritoneal structures and bladder fat away from the vas deferens in a scrotal hernia repair, maintaining hemostasis despite potential bleeding challenges. The dissection progresses deeply to ensure the mesh can sit behind the pubis and cross the midline. The pubic symphysis is visible on the right side, and the dissection continues into the retropubic space to expose the obturator fat plug.

The surgeon leaves the obturator fat plug in place, prioritizing the core for a big indirect hernia repair. The view through the internal ring into the inguinal canal shows no bleeding, avoiding a scrotal hematoma. The corona mortis is visible, and further dissection is performed to reach the obturator space. The mesh is positioned between the bladder, vas deferens, and external iliac vein.

The surgeon places a large 3D Max mesh into the abdominal cavity, ensuring it sits vertically in the retropubic space for optimal posterior lateral coverage. The mesh is positioned across the midline, with corners tucked to prevent displacement by the bladder or hernia. A single 3-0 Vicryl suture is used to secure the mesh to Cooper's ligament, avoiding blindly suturing into the surrounding fat.
